steps.title: The pump screen at the petrol station

narration.default: You've seen it a hundred times — the screen on the petrol pump. The attendant clicks a button, fuel starts flowing, and a number on the screen ticks up and up. Click again, it pauses. Reset, and it goes back to zero. Today you'll build that exact screen — two buttons, one LCD, and a program that knows when to count and when to stop. Make sure Arduino Uno is selected, you're in Upload mode, and the I2C 16-by-2 LCD extension is added in Sensor Manager. If adding extensions is new to you, the Move a Servo tutorial walks you through Sensor Manager.
Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. Same start as every Arduino program — setup runs once, loop runs forever.

Drag a pinMode block into setup. Set pin to 2 and mode to input pullup. Pin 2 will be the Start button — the attendant taps it to start pumping, taps it again to pause. input pullup keeps the reading stable when nothing is pressed, just like in the button-and-LED tutorial.
Drag a second pinMode block below the first. Set pin to 3 and mode to input pullup. Pin 3 will be the Reset button — one press and the total goes back to zero. Two buttons, two pins, both listening.

Here's something strange we'll need to handle. The Start button is just one button. But to make it behave like a real petrol pump, we'll need three separate variables. Three boxes for one button. That sounds like too many — why three? Hold the question. We'll answer it in the next step.

The trick is that one button has to tell us three different things. Fact one — is the pump running right now? That's a yes or no. Fact two — how much fuel has flowed so far? That's a number that grows. Fact three — did the button just transition from not-pressed to pressed? That's edge detection, the same trick from the people-counter tutorial. Three different facts, three different boxes. One pumping flag, one total counter, one lastStart memory of what the button looked like a moment ago.

Drag the setup LCD block into setup, below the two pinModes. The LCD is the little screen on the pump that shows the total. We're using a 16-by-2 LCD — sixteen letters wide, two rows tall — and it talks to the Arduino over a special two-wire connection called I2C, at address hex 27. The block knows the defaults; you just drop it in.

Drag the LCD init block right below. This is the wake-up call — it tells the LCD 'get ready to receive text'. Without this, the screen stays blank no matter what we send. Two LCD setup blocks done; now the screen is alive.

Open the Variables category and click Make a Variable. Name it pumping. Then drag a set pumping to block into setup and set the value to false. This is fact one — is the pump running right now? At the start, no — the pump is idle. So pumping starts as false.

Make another variable, name it total. Drag a set total to block into setup, set the value to 0. This is fact two — how much fuel has flowed. Every loop while the pump is running, this number ticks up. The LCD shows whatever's in this box.

One more variable. Make it, name it lastStart. Drag a set lastStart to block into setup, and drop a high block into its value slot. This is fact three — what did the Start button look like the last time we checked? Because of input pullup, an unpressed button reads high, so we start lastStart at high. We'll use this in a moment to spot the exact instant the button transitions from not-pressed to pressed.

Now into the loop. Drag an if block. In its diamond slot, we want to ask two questions joined by and — is digitalRead pin 2 equal to low right now, and was lastStart equal to high a moment ago? Use an and block from Operators with two equals blocks inside it. Left side — digitalRead 2 equals low. Right side — lastStart equals high. The whole question reads: is the button pressed now, and was it not-pressed a moment ago? That's how we catch a fresh tap and ignore a held finger.

Inside the if branch, drag a set pumping to block. In its value slot, drop a not block from Operators, and inside the not block put a pumping reporter. The whole line reads: set pumping to not pumping. If pumping was false, this flips it to true — the pump starts. If pumping was true, this flips it to false — the pump pauses. One button, toggle behaviour, exactly like a television remote's power key.

Drag a second if block below the first one, still inside the loop. The condition asks: is digitalRead pin 3 equal to low? That's the Reset button being pressed. Inside this if branch, drag a set total to block and set it to 0. One press of Reset, the total goes back to zero — fresh customer, fresh fill-up.

Drag a third if block below. Its condition is just the pumping reporter — true means the pump is running, false means it's idle. Inside this if branch, drag a change total by 1 block. Every loop where pumping is true, total grows by one. Every loop where pumping is false, this branch is skipped and total stays put. That's the difference between flowing and idle, expressed in code.

Drag the LCD print block into the loop, below the three ifs. In its text slot, drop a total reporter. Every loop, the LCD shows whatever number is in the total box right now. When pumping ticks total up, the screen ticks up too. When Reset zeros it, the screen zeros.

steps.title: Save what the button looked like

narration.default: Drag a set lastStart to block at the very bottom of the loop. In its value slot, drop a digitalRead block with pin 2. Every loop, after we've done all our checking, we remember what the Start button looks like right now so the next loop can compare. This is the heartbeat of edge detection — without this line, lastStart never changes, and the fresh-tap check from earlier breaks.

Last block — drag a delay at the bottom of the loop, set to 100 milliseconds. A tenth-of-a-second pause keeps the loop from racing. Every Arduino loop should have a small pause; without one, the program checks the button thousands of times per second and the counter ticks up too fast to read. Blocks are done — let's wire up the simulator.

Open the Simulator tab. Click the plus button at the bottom-right and choose LCD 16x2. The pin dialog asks for sda and scl — leave those at A4 and A5, the standard I2C pins on an Arduino Uno. Click Save. The virtual LCD appears, ready to display whatever your code prints to it.
Click the plus button again, choose Push Button, set pin to 2, click Save. This is the Start button — tap it to begin pumping, tap it again to pause.
One more component. Plus button, Push Button, pin 3, Save. This is the Reset button. Two buttons and an LCD — that's all a real petrol pump screen really is.

Click the Start button at the top of the simulator and give it a few seconds to load. Now press the Start button on the breadboard — the LCD starts ticking up, one count per tenth of a second. Press Start again — it freezes. Press it once more — it ticks up from where it stopped. Press the Reset button — the LCD jumps back to zero. You just built a real-world appliance. Five concepts working together — the pumping flag is a state machine that remembers running-or-idle, the lastStart variable is edge detection that tells a fresh tap apart from a held finger, the change-total-by-one is counter accumulation, the LCD print is screen output, and the delay paces the loop. The same pattern runs every parking meter, every transit-card top-up screen, every coffee-shop loyalty counter you've ever seen.
